obj.title: Can the planet’s coral reefs be saved?
obj.content:
[insert:14][br/]
[p:a]Conservationists have put the final touches to a giant artificial reef they have been assembling at the world-renowned Zoological Society of London (London Zoo). Samples of the planet's most spectacular corals – vivid green branching coral, yellow scroll, blue ridge and many more species – have been added to the giant tank along with fish that thrive in their presence: blue tang, clownfish and many others. The reef is in the zoo's new gallery, Tiny Giants, which is dedicated to the minuscule invertebrate creatures that sustain life across the planet. The coral reef tank and its seven-metre-wide window form the core of the exhibition.[br][br]
'Coral reefs are the most diverse ecosystems on Earth and we want to show people how wonderful they are,' said Paul Pearce-Kelly, senior curator of invertebrates and fish at the Zoological Society of London. 'However, we also want to highlight the research and conservation efforts that are now being carried out to try to save them from the threat of global warming.' They want people to see what is being done to try to save these wonders.[br][br]
[insert:15][br/]
[p:b]Corals are composed of tiny animals, known as polyps, with tentacles for capturing small marine creatures in the sea water. These polyps are transparent but get their brilliant tones of pink, orange, blue, green, etc. from algae that live within them, which in turn get protection, while their photosynthesising of the sun's rays provides nutrients for the polyps. This comfortable symbiotic relationship has led to the growth of coral reefs that cover 0.1% of the planet's ocean bed while providing homes for more than 25% of marine species, including fish, molluscs, sponges and shellfish.[br][br]
[insert:16][br/]
[p:c]As a result, coral reefs are often described as the 'rainforests of the sea', though the comparison is dismissed by some naturalists, including David Attenborough. 'People say you cannot beat the rainforest,' Attenborough has stated. 'But that is simply not true. You go there and the first thing you think is: where are the birds? Where are the animals? They are hiding in the trees, of course. No, if you want beauty and wildlife, you want a coral reef. Put on a mask and stick your head under the water. The sight is mind-blowing.'[br][br]
[insert:17][br/]
[p:d]Unfortunately, these majestic sights are now under very serious threat, with the most immediate problem coming in the form of thermal stress. Rising ocean temperatures are triggering bleaching events that strip reefs of their colour and eventually kill them. And that is just the start. Other menaces include ocean acidification, sea level increase, pollution by humans, deoxygenation and ocean current changes, while the climate crisis is also increasing habitat destruction. As a result, vast areas – including massive chunks of Australia's Great Barrier Reef – have already been destroyed, and scientists advise that more than 90% of reefs could be lost by 2050 unless urgent action is taken to tackle global heating and greenhouse gas emissions.[br][br]
Pearce-Kelly says that coral reefs have to survive really harsh conditions – wave erosion and other factors. And 'when things start to go wrong in the oceans, then corals will be the first to react. And that is exactly what we are seeing now. Coral reefs are dying and they are telling us that all is not well with our planet.'[br][br]
[insert:18][br/]
[p:e]However, scientists are trying to pinpoint hardy types of coral that could survive our overheated oceans, and some of this research will be carried out at London Zoo. 'Behind our … coral reef tank we have built laboratories where scientists will be studying coral species,' said Pearce-Kelly. One aim will be to carry out research on species to find those that can survive best in warm, acidic waters. Another will be to try to increase coral breeding rates. 'Coral spawn just once a year,' he added. 'However, aquarium-based research has enabled some corals to spawn artificially, which can assist coral reef restoration efforts. And if this can be extended for all species, we could consider the launching of coral-spawning programmes several times a year. That would be a big help in restoring blighted reefs.'[br][br]
[insert:19][br/]
[p:f]Research in these fields is being conducted in laboratories around the world, with the London Zoo centre linked to this global network. Studies carried out in one centre can then be tested in others. The resulting young coral can then be displayed in the tank in Tiny Giants. 'The crucial point is that the progress we make in making coral better able to survive in a warming world can be shown to the public and encourage them to believe that we can do something to save the planet's reefs,' said Pearce-Kelly. 'Saving our coral reefs is now a critically important ecological goal.'
obj.content_zh:
[br/]
[b] A. [/b]环保主义者近日为伦敦动物园内建造的大型人工珊瑚礁完成了最后工序。这座世界知名动物园的新展馆"微小巨人"中，七米宽的观景窗前，一个巨型水族箱里引入了地球上最绚丽的珊瑚样本--翠绿的分枝珊瑚、黄色的卷曲珊瑚、蓝色的脊状珊瑚等众多品种，以及与之共生的鱼类:蓝倒吊鱼、小丑鱼等。该展馆专门展示维系地球生命的无脊椎微小生物，珊瑚礁水族箱是其核心展项[br][br]
"珊瑚礁是地球上最多样化的生态系统，我们想向人们展示它们的奇妙，"伦敦动物园无脊椎动物和鱼类高级策展人保罗·皮尔斯-凯利表示，"但更重要的是揭示当前为拯救它们免受全球变暖威胁所开展的研究和保护工作。"他们希望公众了解这些自然奇观的抢救进展。[br][br]
[br/]
[b] B. [/b]珊瑚由名为珊瑚虫的微小动物构成，这些带触手的生物通过捕捉海水中的微生物为生。珊瑚虫本身透明，其呈现的粉、橙、蓝、绿等艳丽色彩源自体内共生的藻类--藻类获得庇护所的同时通过光合作用为珊瑚虫提供养分。这种完美的共生关系造就了覆盖全球海床0.1%的珊瑚礁，却为超过25%的海洋物种(包括鱼类、软体动物、海绵和贝类)提供栖息地。[br][br]
[br/]
[b] C. [/b]因此珊瑚礁常被称为"海洋雨林"，但包括大卫·爱登堡在内的自然学家认为这种类比并不恰当。"人们总说雨林无可比拟，"爱登堡指出，"但这并非事实。当你亲临雨林，首先疑惑的是:鸟兽都藏在哪里?它们当然躲在树丛中。若论生物多样性的直观呈现，珊瑚礁才是终极选择--只需戴上面罩将头浸入水中，震撼景象便扑面而来。[br][br]
[br/]
[b] D. [/b]遗憾的是，这些壮丽景观正面临严峻威胁，最迫在眉睫的是热应激反应。海水温度上升导致珊瑚白化事件频发，最终使礁体死亡。这仅是开端，其他威胁还包括海洋酸化、海平面上升、人为污染、海水脱氧和洋流变化，而气候危机更在加剧栖息地破坏。澳大利亚大堡礁等大面积礁体已经消亡，科学家警告若不立即采取行动应对全球变暖和温室气体排放，到2050年超过90%的珊瑚礁或将消失。[br][br]
皮尔斯-凯利指出，珊瑚礁本就承受着海浪侵蚀等严酷自然考验，"当海洋生态系统开始失衡，珊瑚总是最先发出警报。我们此刻目睹的珊瑚大规模死亡，正是地球生态恶化的明确信号。”[br][br]
[br/]
[b]E. [/b]科学家正试图筛选能适应变暖海洋的顽强珊瑚品种，部分研究将在伦敦动物园开展。"珊瑚礁水族箱后方我们建立了实验室，"皮尔斯-凯利介绍道。研究目标包括筛选耐高温酸性水体的珊瑚物种，以及提升珊瑚繁殖率。"珊瑚每年仅产卵一次，"他补充说，"但水族馆研究已实现部分品种人工产卵，这有助于珊瑚礁修复。若技术能推广至所有物种，我们或可实现每年多次珊瑚产卵计划，这对修复受损礁体将大有裨益。"[br][br]
[br/]
[b]F. [/b]相关研究正在全球实验室网络中展开，伦敦动物园中心与之紧密联动。某地的研究成果可在其他机构验证，培育的幼珊瑚则能在"微小巨人"展馆展示。"关键在于，我们提升珊瑚适应暖化环境的研究进展能直观呈现给公众，"皮尔斯-凯利强调，"这能增强人们拯救珊瑚礁的信心。保护珊瑚礁已成为当今最紧迫的生态目标之一。
obj.html_content:
[insert:14]

aConservationists have put the final touches to a giant artificial reef they have been assembling at the world-renowned Zoological Society of London (London Zoo). Samples of the planet's most spectacular corals – vivid green branching coral, yellow scroll, blue ridge and many more species – have been added to the giant tank along with fish that thrive in their presence: blue tang, clownfish and many others. The reef is in the zoo's new gallery, Tiny Giants, which is dedicated to the minuscule invertebrate creatures that sustain life across the planet. The coral reef tank and its seven-metre-wide window form the core of the exhibition.

'Coral reefs are the most diverse ecosystems on Earth and we want to show people how wonderful they are,' said Paul Pearce-Kelly, senior curator of invertebrates and fish at the Zoological Society of London. 'However, we also want to highlight the research and conservation efforts that are now being carried out to try to save them from the threat of global warming.' They want people to see what is being done to try to save these wonders.

[insert:15]

bCorals are composed of tiny animals, known as polyps, with tentacles for capturing small marine creatures in the sea water. These polyps are transparent but get their brilliant tones of pink, orange, blue, green, etc. from algae that live within them, which in turn get protection, while their photosynthesising of the sun's rays provides nutrients for the polyps. This comfortable symbiotic relationship has led to the growth of coral reefs that cover 0.1% of the planet's ocean bed while providing homes for more than 25% of marine species, including fish, molluscs, sponges and shellfish.

[insert:16]

cAs a result, coral reefs are often described as the 'rainforests of the sea', though the comparison is dismissed by some naturalists, including David Attenborough. 'People say you cannot beat the rainforest,' Attenborough has stated. 'But that is simply not true. You go there and the first thing you think is: where are the birds? Where are the animals? They are hiding in the trees, of course. No, if you want beauty and wildlife, you want a coral reef. Put on a mask and stick your head under the water. The sight is mind-blowing.'

[insert:17]

dUnfortunately, these majestic sights are now under very serious threat, with the most immediate problem coming in the form of thermal stress. Rising ocean temperatures are triggering bleaching events that strip reefs of their colour and eventually kill them. And that is just the start. Other menaces include ocean acidification, sea level increase, pollution by humans, deoxygenation and ocean current changes, while the climate crisis is also increasing habitat destruction. As a result, vast areas – including massive chunks of Australia's Great Barrier Reef – have already been destroyed, and scientists advise that more than 90% of reefs could be lost by 2050 unless urgent action is taken to tackle global heating and greenhouse gas emissions.

Pearce-Kelly says that coral reefs have to survive really harsh conditions – wave erosion and other factors. And 'when things start to go wrong in the oceans, then corals will be the first to react. And that is exactly what we are seeing now. Coral reefs are dying and they are telling us that all is not well with our planet.'

[insert:18]

eHowever, scientists are trying to pinpoint hardy types of coral that could survive our overheated oceans, and some of this research will be carried out at London Zoo. 'Behind our … coral reef tank we have built laboratories where scientists will be studying coral species,' said Pearce-Kelly. One aim will be to carry out research on species to find those that can survive best in warm, acidic waters. Another will be to try to increase coral breeding rates. 'Coral spawn just once a year,' he added. 'However, aquarium-based research has enabled some corals to spawn artificially, which can assist coral reef restoration efforts. And if this can be extended for all species, we could consider the launching of coral-spawning programmes several times a year. That would be a big help in restoring blighted reefs.'

[insert:19]

fResearch in these fields is being conducted in laboratories around the world, with the London Zoo centre linked to this global network. Studies carried out in one centre can then be tested in others. The resulting young coral can then be displayed in the tank in Tiny Giants. 'The crucial point is that the progress we make in making coral better able to survive in a warming world can be shown to the public and encourage them to believe that we can do something to save the planet's reefs,' said Pearce-Kelly. 'Saving our coral reefs is now a critically important ecological goal.'

added_content.ex_information:
Choose
TWO
letters,
A–E
.

obj.content: Which [b] TWO [/b] of these causes of damage to coral reefs are mentioned by the writer of the text?
obj.html_content: Which TWO of these causes of damage to coral reefs are mentioned by the writer of the text?

obj.content: a rising number of extreme storms
obj.html_content: a rising number of extreme storms

obj.content: the removal of too many fish from the sea
obj.html_content: the removal of too many fish from the sea

obj.content: the contamination of the sea from waste
obj.html_content: the contamination of the sea from waste

obj.content: increased disease among marine species
obj.html_content: increased disease among marine species

obj.content: alterations in the usual flow of water in the seas
obj.html_content: alterations in the usual flow of water in the seas

[insert:14]

aConservationists have put the final touches to a giant artificial reef they have been assembling at the world-renowned Zoological Society of London (London Zoo). [insert:15]

bCorals are composed of tiny animals, known as polyps, with tentacles for capturing small marine creatures in the sea water. These polyps are transparent but get their brilliant tones of pink, orange, blue, green, etc. from algae that live within them, which in turn get protection, while their photosynthesising of the sun's rays provides nutrients for the polyps. This comfortable symbiotic relationship has led to the growth of coral reefs that cover 0.1% of the planet's ocean bed while providing homes for more than 25% of marine species, including fish, molluscs, sponges and shellfish.

[insert:16]

cAs a result, coral reefs are often described as the 'rainforests of the sea', though the comparison is dismissed by some naturalists, including David Attenborough. 'People say you cannot beat the rainforest,' Attenborough has stated. 'But that is simply not true. You go there and the first thing you think is: where are the birds? Where are the animals? They are hiding in the trees, of course. No, if you want beauty and wildlife, you want a coral reef. Put on a mask and stick your head under the water. The sight is mind-blowing.'

[insert:17]

dUnfortunately, these majestic sights are now under very serious threat, with the most immediate problem coming in the form of thermal stress. Rising ocean temperatures are triggering bleaching events that strip reefs of their colour and eventually kill them. And that is just the start. Other menaces include ocean acidification, sea level increase, pollution by humans, deoxygenation and ocean current changes, while the climate crisis is also increasing habitat destruction. As a result, vast areas – including massive chunks of Australia's Great Barrier Reef – have already been destroyed, and scientists advise that more than 90% of reefs could be lost by 2050 unless urgent action is taken to tackle global heating and greenhouse gas emissions.

Pearce-Kelly says that coral reefs have to survive really harsh conditions – wave erosion and other factors. And 'when things start to go wrong in the oceans, then corals will be the first to react. And that is exactly what we are seeing now. Coral reefs are dying and they are telling us that all is not well with our planet.'

[insert:18]

eHowever, scientists are trying to pinpoint hardy types of coral that could survive our overheated oceans, and some of this research will be carried out at London Zoo. 'Behind our … coral reef tank we have built laboratories where scientists will be studying coral species,' said Pearce-Kelly. One aim will be to carry out research on species to find those that can survive best in warm, acidic waters. Another will be to try to increase coral breeding rates. 'Coral spawn just once a year,' he added. 'However, aquarium-based research has enabled some corals to spawn artificially, which can assist coral reef restoration efforts. And if this can be extended for all species, we could consider the launching of coral-spawning programmes several times a year. That would be a big help in restoring blighted reefs.'

[insert:19]

fResearch in these fields is being conducted in laboratories around the world, with the London Zoo centre linked to this global network. Studies carried out in one centre can then be tested in others. The resulting young coral can then be displayed in the tank in Tiny Giants. 'The crucial point is that the progress we make in making coral better able to survive in a warming world can be shown to the public and encourage them to believe that we can do something to save the planet's reefs,' said Pearce-Kelly. 'Saving our coral reefs is now a critically important ecological goal.'

obj.content: Which [b] TWO [/b] of the following statements are true of the researchers at London Zoo?

obj.content: They are hoping to expand the numbers of different corals being bred in laboratories.

obj.content: They want to identify corals that can cope well with the changed sea conditions.

obj.title: Can the planet’s coral reefs be saved?
obj.content:
[insert:14][br/]
[p:a]Conservationists have put the final touches to a giant artificial reef they have been assembling at the world-renowned Zoological Society of London (London Zoo). Samples of the planet's most spectacular corals – vivid green branching coral, yellow scroll, blue ridge and many more species – have been added to the giant tank along with fish that thrive in their presence: blue tang, clownfish and many others. The reef is in the zoo's new gallery, Tiny Giants, which is dedicated to the minuscule invertebrate creatures that sustain life across the planet. The coral reef tank and its seven-metre-wide window form the core of the exhibition.[br][br]
'Coral reefs are the most diverse ecosystems on Earth and we want to show people how wonderful they are,' said Paul Pearce-Kelly, senior curator of invertebrates and fish at the Zoological Society of London. 'However, we also want to highlight the research and conservation efforts that are now being carried out to try to save them from the threat of global warming.' They want people to see what is being done to try to save these wonders.[br][br]
[insert:15][br/]
[p:b]Corals are composed of tiny animals, known as polyps, with tentacles for capturing small marine creatures in the sea water. These polyps are transparent but get their brilliant tones of pink, orange, blue, green, etc. from algae that live within them, which in turn get protection, while their photosynthesising of the sun's rays provides nutrients for the polyps. This comfortable symbiotic relationship has led to the growth of coral reefs that cover 0.1% of the planet's ocean bed while providing homes for more than 25% of marine species, including fish, molluscs, sponges and shellfish.[br][br]
[insert:16][br/]
[p:c]As a result, coral reefs are often described as the 'rainforests of the sea', though the comparison is dismissed by some naturalists, including David Attenborough. 'People say you cannot beat the rainforest,' Attenborough has stated. 'But that is simply not true. You go there and the first thing you think is: where are the birds? Where are the animals? They are hiding in the trees, of course. No, if you want beauty and wildlife, you want a coral reef. Put on a mask and stick your head under the water. The sight is mind-blowing.'[br][br]
[insert:17][br/]
[p:d]Unfortunately, these majestic sights are now under very serious threat, with the most immediate problem coming in the form of thermal stress. Rising ocean temperatures are triggering bleaching events that strip reefs of their colour and eventually kill them. And that is just the start. Other menaces include ocean acidification, sea level increase, pollution by humans, deoxygenation and ocean current changes, while the climate crisis is also increasing habitat destruction. As a result, vast areas – including massive chunks of Australia's Great Barrier Reef – have already been destroyed, and scientists advise that more than 90% of reefs could be lost by 2050 unless urgent action is taken to tackle global heating and greenhouse gas emissions.[br][br]
Pearce-Kelly says that coral reefs have to survive really harsh conditions – wave erosion and other factors. And 'when things start to go wrong in the oceans, then corals will be the first to react. And that is exactly what we are seeing now. Coral reefs are dying and they are telling us that all is not well with our planet.'[br][br]
[insert:18][br/]
[p:e]However, scientists are trying to pinpoint hardy types of coral that could survive our overheated oceans, and some of this research will be carried out at London Zoo. 'Behind our … coral reef tank we have built laboratories where scientists will be studying coral species,' said Pearce-Kelly. One aim will be to carry out research on species to find those that can survive best in warm, acidic waters. Another will be to try to increase coral breeding rates. 'Coral spawn just once a year,' he added. 'However, aquarium-based research has enabled some corals to spawn artificially, which can assist coral reef restoration efforts. And if this can be extended for all species, we could consider the launching of coral-spawning programmes several times a year. That would be a big help in restoring blighted reefs.'[br][br]
[insert:19][br/]
[p:f]Research in these fields is being conducted in laboratories around the world, with the London Zoo centre linked to this global network. Studies carried out in one centre can then be tested in others. The resulting young coral can then be displayed in the tank in Tiny Giants. 'The crucial point is that the progress we make in making coral better able to survive in a warming world can be shown to the public and encourage them to believe that we can do something to save the planet's reefs,' said Pearce-Kelly. 'Saving our coral reefs is now a critically important ecological goal.'
obj.content_zh:
[br/]
[b] A. [/b]环保主义者近日为伦敦动物园内建造的大型人工珊瑚礁完成了最后工序。这座世界知名动物园的新展馆"微小巨人"中，七米宽的观景窗前，一个巨型水族箱里引入了地球上最绚丽的珊瑚样本--翠绿的分枝珊瑚、黄色的卷曲珊瑚、蓝色的脊状珊瑚等众多品种，以及与之共生的鱼类:蓝倒吊鱼、小丑鱼等。该展馆专门展示维系地球生命的无脊椎微小生物，珊瑚礁水族箱是其核心展项[br][br]
"珊瑚礁是地球上最多样化的生态系统，我们想向人们展示它们的奇妙，"伦敦动物园无脊椎动物和鱼类高级策展人保罗·皮尔斯-凯利表示，"但更重要的是揭示当前为拯救它们免受全球变暖威胁所开展的研究和保护工作。"他们希望公众了解这些自然奇观的抢救进展。[br][br]
[br/]
[b] B. [/b]珊瑚由名为珊瑚虫的微小动物构成，这些带触手的生物通过捕捉海水中的微生物为生。珊瑚虫本身透明，其呈现的粉、橙、蓝、绿等艳丽色彩源自体内共生的藻类--藻类获得庇护所的同时通过光合作用为珊瑚虫提供养分。这种完美的共生关系造就了覆盖全球海床0.1%的珊瑚礁，却为超过25%的海洋物种(包括鱼类、软体动物、海绵和贝类)提供栖息地。[br][br]
[br/]
[b] C. [/b]因此珊瑚礁常被称为"海洋雨林"，但包括大卫·爱登堡在内的自然学家认为这种类比并不恰当。"人们总说雨林无可比拟，"爱登堡指出，"但这并非事实。当你亲临雨林，首先疑惑的是:鸟兽都藏在哪里?它们当然躲在树丛中。若论生物多样性的直观呈现，珊瑚礁才是终极选择--只需戴上面罩将头浸入水中，震撼景象便扑面而来。[br][br]
[br/]
[b] D. [/b]遗憾的是，这些壮丽景观正面临严峻威胁，最迫在眉睫的是热应激反应。海水温度上升导致珊瑚白化事件频发，最终使礁体死亡。这仅是开端，其他威胁还包括海洋酸化、海平面上升、人为污染、海水脱氧和洋流变化，而气候危机更在加剧栖息地破坏。澳大利亚大堡礁等大面积礁体已经消亡，科学家警告若不立即采取行动应对全球变暖和温室气体排放，到2050年超过90%的珊瑚礁或将消失。[br][br]
皮尔斯-凯利指出，珊瑚礁本就承受着海浪侵蚀等严酷自然考验，"当海洋生态系统开始失衡，珊瑚总是最先发出警报。我们此刻目睹的珊瑚大规模死亡，正是地球生态恶化的明确信号。”[br][br]
[br/]
[b]E. [/b]科学家正试图筛选能适应变暖海洋的顽强珊瑚品种，部分研究将在伦敦动物园开展。"珊瑚礁水族箱后方我们建立了实验室，"皮尔斯-凯利介绍道。研究目标包括筛选耐高温酸性水体的珊瑚物种，以及提升珊瑚繁殖率。"珊瑚每年仅产卵一次，"他补充说，"但水族馆研究已实现部分品种人工产卵，这有助于珊瑚礁修复。若技术能推广至所有物种，我们或可实现每年多次珊瑚产卵计划，这对修复受损礁体将大有裨益。"[br][br]
[br/]
[b]F. [/b]相关研究正在全球实验室网络中展开，伦敦动物园中心与之紧密联动。某地的研究成果可在其他机构验证，培育的幼珊瑚则能在"微小巨人"展馆展示。"关键在于，我们提升珊瑚适应暖化环境的研究进展能直观呈现给公众，"皮尔斯-凯利强调，"这能增强人们拯救珊瑚礁的信心。保护珊瑚礁已成为当今最紧迫的生态目标之一。
obj.html_content:
[insert:14]

aConservationists have put the final touches to a giant artificial reef they have been assembling at the world-renowned Zoological Society of London (London Zoo). Samples of the planet's most spectacular corals – vivid green branching coral, yellow scroll, blue ridge and many more species – have been added to the giant tank along with fish that thrive in their presence: blue tang, clownfish and many others. The reef is in the zoo's new gallery, Tiny Giants, which is dedicated to the minuscule invertebrate creatures that sustain life across the planet. The coral reef tank and its seven-metre-wide window form the core of the exhibition.

'Coral reefs are the most diverse ecosystems on Earth and we want to show people how wonderful they are,' said Paul Pearce-Kelly, senior curator of invertebrates and fish at the Zoological Society of London. 'However, we also want to highlight the research and conservation efforts that are now being carried out to try to save them from the threat of global warming.' They want people to see what is being done to try to save these wonders.

[insert:15]

bCorals are composed of tiny animals, known as polyps, with tentacles for capturing small marine creatures in the sea water. These polyps are transparent but get their brilliant tones of pink, orange, blue, green, etc. from algae that live within them, which in turn get protection, while their photosynthesising of the sun's rays provides nutrients for the polyps. This comfortable symbiotic relationship has led to the growth of coral reefs that cover 0.1% of the planet's ocean bed while providing homes for more than 25% of marine species, including fish, molluscs, sponges and shellfish.

[insert:16]

cAs a result, coral reefs are often described as the 'rainforests of the sea', though the comparison is dismissed by some naturalists, including David Attenborough. 'People say you cannot beat the rainforest,' Attenborough has stated. 'But that is simply not true. You go there and the first thing you think is: where are the birds? Where are the animals? They are hiding in the trees, of course. No, if you want beauty and wildlife, you want a coral reef. Put on a mask and stick your head under the water. The sight is mind-blowing.'

[insert:17]

dUnfortunately, these majestic sights are now under very serious threat, with the most immediate problem coming in the form of thermal stress. Rising ocean temperatures are triggering bleaching events that strip reefs of their colour and eventually kill them. And that is just the start. Other menaces include ocean acidification, sea level increase, pollution by humans, deoxygenation and ocean current changes, while the climate crisis is also increasing habitat destruction. As a result, vast areas – including massive chunks of Australia's Great Barrier Reef – have already been destroyed, and scientists advise that more than 90% of reefs could be lost by 2050 unless urgent action is taken to tackle global heating and greenhouse gas emissions.

Pearce-Kelly says that coral reefs have to survive really harsh conditions – wave erosion and other factors. And 'when things start to go wrong in the oceans, then corals will be the first to react. And that is exactly what we are seeing now. Coral reefs are dying and they are telling us that all is not well with our planet.'

[insert:18]

eHowever, scientists are trying to pinpoint hardy types of coral that could survive our overheated oceans, and some of this research will be carried out at London Zoo. 'Behind our … coral reef tank we have built laboratories where scientists will be studying coral species,' said Pearce-Kelly. One aim will be to carry out research on species to find those that can survive best in warm, acidic waters. Another will be to try to increase coral breeding rates. 'Coral spawn just once a year,' he added. 'However, aquarium-based research has enabled some corals to spawn artificially, which can assist coral reef restoration efforts. And if this can be extended for all species, we could consider the launching of coral-spawning programmes several times a year. That would be a big help in restoring blighted reefs.'

[insert:19]

fResearch in these fields is being conducted in laboratories around the world, with the London Zoo centre linked to this global network. Studies carried out in one centre can then be tested in others. The resulting young coral can then be displayed in the tank in Tiny Giants. 'The crucial point is that the progress we make in making coral better able to survive in a warming world can be shown to the public and encourage them to believe that we can do something to save the planet's reefs,' said Pearce-Kelly. 'Saving our coral reefs is now a critically important ecological goal.'

皮尔斯-凯利指出，珊瑚礁本就承受着海浪侵蚀等严酷自然考验，"当海洋生态系统开始失衡，珊瑚总是最先发出警报。我们此刻目睹的珊瑚大规模死亡，正是地球生态恶化的明确信号。”[br][br]
[br/]
[b]E. [/b]科学家正试图筛选能适应变暖海洋的顽强珊瑚品种，部分研究将在伦敦动物园开展。"珊瑚礁水族箱后方我们建立了实验室，"皮尔斯-凯利介绍道。研究目标包括筛选耐高温酸性水体的珊瑚物种，以及提升珊瑚繁殖率。"珊瑚每年仅产卵一次，"他补充说，"但水族馆研究已实现部分品种人工产卵，这有助于珊瑚礁修复。若技术能推广至所有物种，我们或可实现每年多次珊瑚产卵计划，这对修复受损礁体将大有裨益。"[br][br]
[br/]
[b]F. [/b]相关研究正在全球实验室网络中展开，伦敦动物园中心与之紧密联动。某地的研究成果可在其他机构验证，培育的幼珊瑚则能在"微小巨人"展馆展示。"关键在于，我们提升珊瑚适应暖化环境的研究进展能直观呈现给公众，"皮尔斯-凯利强调，"这能增强人们拯救珊瑚礁的信心。保护珊瑚礁已成为当今最紧迫的生态目标之一。
obj.html_content:
[insert:14]

aConservationists have put the final touches to a giant artificial reef they have been assembling at the world-renowned Zoological Society of London (London Zoo). Samples of the planet's most spectacular corals – vivid green branching coral, yellow scroll, blue ridge and many more species – have been added to the giant tank along with fish that thrive in their presence: blue tang, clownfish and many others. The reef is in the zoo's new gallery, Tiny Giants, which is dedicated to the minuscule invertebrate creatures that sustain life across the planet. The coral reef tank and its seven-metre-wide window form the core of the exhibition.

'Coral reefs are the most diverse ecosystems on Earth and we want to show people how wonderful they are,' said Paul Pearce-Kelly, senior curator of invertebrates and fish at the Zoological Society of London. 'However, we also want to highlight the research and conservation efforts that are now being carried out to try to save them from the threat of global warming.' They want people to see what is being done to try to save these wonders.

[insert:15]

bCorals are composed of tiny animals, known as polyps, with tentacles for capturing small marine creatures in the sea water. These polyps are transparent but get their brilliant tones of pink, orange, blue, green, etc. from algae that live within them, which in turn get protection, while their photosynthesising of the sun's rays provides nutrients for the polyps. This comfortable symbiotic relationship has led to the growth of coral reefs that cover 0.1% of the planet's ocean bed while providing homes for more than 25% of marine species, including fish, molluscs, sponges and shellfish.

[insert:16]

cAs a result, coral reefs are often described as the 'rainforests of the sea', though the comparison is dismissed by some naturalists, including David Attenborough. 'People say you cannot beat the rainforest,' Attenborough has stated. 'But that is simply not true. You go there and the first thing you think is: where are the birds? Where are the animals? They are hiding in the trees, of course. No, if you want beauty and wildlife, you want a coral reef. Put on a mask and stick your head under the water. The sight is mind-blowing.'

[insert:17]

dUnfortunately, these majestic sights are now under very serious threat, with the most immediate problem coming in the form of thermal stress. Rising ocean temperatures are triggering bleaching events that strip reefs of their colour and eventually kill them. And that is just the start. Other menaces include ocean acidification, sea level increase, pollution by humans, deoxygenation and ocean current changes, while the climate crisis is also increasing habitat destruction. As a result, vast areas – including massive chunks of Australia's Great Barrier Reef – have already been destroyed, and scientists advise that more than 90% of reefs could be lost by 2050 unless urgent action is taken to tackle global heating and greenhouse gas emissions.

Pearce-Kelly says that coral reefs have to survive really harsh conditions – wave erosion and other factors. And 'when things start to go wrong in the oceans, then corals will be the first to react. And that is exactly what we are seeing now. Coral reefs are dying and they are telling us that all is not well with our planet.'

[insert:18]

eHowever, scientists are trying to pinpoint hardy types of coral that could survive our overheated oceans, and some of this research will be carried out at London Zoo. 'Behind our … coral reef tank we have built laboratories where scientists will be studying coral species,' said Pearce-Kelly. One aim will be to carry out research on species to find those that can survive best in warm, acidic waters. Another will be to try to increase coral breeding rates. 'Coral spawn just once a year,' he added. 'However, aquarium-based research has enabled some corals to spawn artificially, which can assist coral reef restoration efforts. And if this can be extended for all species, we could consider the launching of coral-spawning programmes several times a year. That would be a big help in restoring blighted reefs.'

[insert:19]

fResearch in these fields is being conducted in laboratories around the world, with the London Zoo centre linked to this global network. Studies carried out in one centre can then be tested in others. The resulting young coral can then be displayed in the tank in Tiny Giants. 'The crucial point is that the progress we make in making coral better able to survive in a warming world can be shown to the public and encourage them to believe that we can do something to save the planet's reefs,' said Pearce-Kelly. 'Saving our coral reefs is now a critically important ecological goal.'

sectionSheet.title: Can the planet’s coral reefs be saved?

officialExplain.explain:
本部分包括两个段落，其中第一段讲了conservationists完成了giant artificial reef的项目，在第二个段落中，明确给出了进行这一项目的目的，一方面是为了show people how wonderful they are，另一方面是为了highlight the research and conservation efforts that are now being carried out to try to save them from the threat of global warming。基于以上内容，在选项中能给概括这部分信息的是Two clear educational goals。

因此本题答案为E。

本段主要描述了珊瑚和藻类的共生关系，珊瑚虫本身是透明的，但是通过藻类可以get their brilliant tones of pink, orange, blue, green, etc，而藻类也因为live inside them而获得protection，同时还可以provides nutrients for the polyps，之后本段又写道这种comfortable symbiotic relationship所带来的积极结果，因此可以通过Cooperation beneath the waves来概括总结本段内容，这也就可以作为本段的标题。

因此本题答案为B。

本段首先说道，coral reefs 经常被叫做rainforests of the sea，之后本段主要内容都是在讲解为什么that is simply not true，也就是说认为这种表述是不准确的，这与选项中disagreement about the accuracy of a certain phrase的表述一致，因此这一选项可以作为本段标题。

因此本题答案为D。

本段开头便指出，these majestic sights are now under very serious threat，之后对thermal stress这一具体问题进行了描述，随后，又提出了更多形式threats，比如ocean acidification, sea level increase, pollution by humans, deoxygenation and ocean current changes，并且climate crisis会带来habitat destruction。根据以上内容，综合本段的内容，可以总结出本段主题为A warning of further trouble ahead。

因此本题答案为G。

本段主要描述为解决珊瑚面临的威胁而做出的努力，第一句便通过however的转折和之后的trying to pinpoint hardy types of coral that could survive our overheated oceans，来与上一个段落内容进行衔接，在随后又做了具体的描述，并指出当前努力的目的，一方面是为了carry out research on species to find those that can survive best in warm, acidic waters，另一方面是为了increase coral breeding rates，这些做法都是为了缓解珊瑚面临的问题。

因此本题答案为C。

本段延续上一段的内容，首先描述了当前研究成果如何实现共享，其后又说道这些进展can be shown to the public and encourage them to believe that we can do something to save the planet’s reefs，由此可见通过现有的成果，可以让大家对save the planet’s reefs更有信心，这与选项中promoting hope的信息是一致的。

因此本题答案为F。

译文：作者在文中提及了哪两个珊瑚礁受损的原因？

选项A：a rising number of extreme storms（极端风暴数量增加）

文中未提及extreme storms的增加对coral reefs的影响。

选项B： the removal of too many fish from the sea（从海洋中捕捞过多鱼类）

文中未提及the removal of too many fish from the sea对coral reefs的影响。

选项C： the contamination of the sea from waste（废弃物导致海洋污染）

在文章D段提到的多个对coral reef的威胁中，包括pollution by humans这一信息，这也就是本选项的同义替换。

选项D： increased disease among marine species（海洋物种疾病增多）

文中未提及disease among marine species对coral reefs的影响。

选项E：alterations in the usual flow of water in the seas（海水常规流动模式改变）

在文章D段提到的多个对coral reef的威胁中，包括ocean current changes这一信息，这也就是本选项的同义替换。

译文：以下哪两个关于伦敦动物园研究人员的陈述是正确的？

选项A：They are hoping to expand the numbers of different corals being bred in laboratories.（他们希望扩大实验室培育的不同珊瑚种类数量）

文中关于researchers at London Zoo的描述中，未提及本信息。

选项B：They want to identify corals that can cope well with the changed sea conditions.（他们想要识别能够很好适应变化的海洋条件的珊瑚）

文章E段提到，to find those that can survive best in warm, acidic waters.，这与本选项的信息一致。

选项C：They are looking at ways of creating artificial reefs that corals could grow on.（他们正在研究建造可供珊瑚生长的人工礁石的方法）

文中关于researchers at London Zoo的描述中，未提及本信息。

选项D：They are trying out methods that would speed up reproduction in some corals. （他们正在试验能够加速某些珊瑚繁殖的方法）

文章E段提到，to increase coral breeding rates，这与本选项的信息一致。

选项E：They are investigating materials that might protect reefs from higher temperatures.（他们正在研究可能保护珊瑚礁免受高温影响的材料）

文中关于researchers at London Zoo的描述中，未提及本信息。

B段第一句：Corals are composed of tiny animals, known as polyps, with tentacles for capturing small marine creatures in the sea water.

译文：珊瑚拥有许多______，可以用于收集食物。

本题考查的是corals通过什么来collect food，从文中B段第一句可以看到with tentacles for capturing small marine creatures in the sea water，因此corals通过tentacles来获取食物。

因此本题答案为tentacles。

B段第二句：These polyps are transparent but get their brilliant tones of pink, orange, blue, green, etc. from algae that live within them, which in turn get protection, while their photosynthesising of the sun’s rays provides nutrients for the polyps.

译文：藻类通过位于珊瑚体内获得______。

本题对应文中B段第二句，针对corals和algae的共生关系进行考查，题目中being inside the coral与from algae that live within them的表述一致，随后说到which in turn get protection，也就是说这种共生状态可以使algae得到保护。

因此本题答案为protection。

D段第二句：Rising ocean temperatures are triggering bleaching events that strip reefs of their colour and eventually kill them.

题干：Increases in the warmth of the sea water can remove the ______ from coral.

译文：海水温度上升会夺走珊瑚的______。

题目中increases in the warmth of the sea water与文章D段第二句开头的rising ocean temperatures同义替换，题目中的remove与文中strip同义替换，因此答案对应的就是文中strip reefs of their colour这一信息。

因此本题答案为colour。
